speaker: interviewer
text: You were just telling me that you got married. But before that, when did you find out what happened to your family? To your brothers? To your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. When I came back I asked the town people what happened? You know anything about my family? So they told us what happened. One day they-- they took all the people to the temple and then a couple days or a week or so they took at the [INAUDIBLE] the [INAUDIBLE] we had from them. They took them then to concentration camp. Not anything from my family. Nobody knows anything specifically from my family but they-- they were talking generally what happened. Then was waiting there when people came home somewhere. From-- first was the came back the people from-- from the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: They couldn't give me any information, and we couldn't get anything. Then later on people came back from concentration camp, and I asked them, did you know-- do you know-- do you know? Do you remember this and this and nobody could tell me anything.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, many years, later and I-- then I find somebody, a woman, who said, I was with your sister and with her daughter. She said your-- her daughter was very sick and one day she died. And then couple days after, your sister died. She couldn't remember where was, how was. That's-- that's all he could tell me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which sister? Which sister was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ilonna?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilonna, yeah. Later then I met some gentleman. So those-- those years whenever you met somebody who came back from concentration, you-- you ask question. Where were you? Did you know anything from my family? So then I met one-- one gentleman who said I was with your brother in-law, Katz. Samuel Katz. He was a tall man. He was a strong, tall man. He said, we were-- we came back from work one day, one evening, and we were sitting somewhere on a step, and we are talking. And he suddenly just fall down and drop dead. That's all. Then when my brother from my other sister, from the-- she not-- they never heard anything what happened with them. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Yeah. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] Then from other relatives, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some of them I heard what happened and what-- but not really. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS] Couple years ago, I was in Bratislava with my son, and there in the restaurant, there was a kosher restaurant over there, I met a lady. And we got talking. She was-- she was-- was a survivor, and I was asking because then nearby I had an aunt-- aunt and uncle. And I mentioned this name, [INAUDIBLE]. Yeah, I was the-- I was with her. What happened with her she didn't know, but I was with her, she tells. Yeah. So that's all. What I heard. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So how big was your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother, there were six sisters and one brother. My father was three brothers and one sister. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them, the older people, did die before the war. But the children, grandchildren, they were-- they were [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: How many did not survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's over 50, whole family [INAUDIBLE] more than 50 people. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Now, how many survived? You said, now, how many survived? Not many. But my cousins-- some of my cousins' children survives, five or six. But about more than 50 did-- didn't survived, in my family.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were telling me about getting married.

speaker: interviewee
text: What?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were just going to tell me, before, how you got married. Could you describe that? How you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I married. Yeah, well, I came back. So I would think about it. I'm-- I'd like to marry, settle down, and have-- make a-- have a family-- children. And when Ilona came-- came, and I-- I felt she's the right woman, what I would like to spend the rest of my life. Somehow, it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ilona, Ilona [? Engel. ?] That was her-- that's his name-- her name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know her from before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I know her. I know her as a little girl, a young girl. But I-- I wasn't-- it didn't interest me. Well, I was seven years older. When I was 20, she was 13. So a 20 boy, who cares about 13? And then in 1940, when I went to the labor camp, and I haven't seen her more.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what date did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1946, February 12.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did the people of Tokaj--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Tokaj, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the people in Tokaj behave towards survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Behaved, what?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they-- how did the citizens, the non-Jews, behave towards--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, they-- we didn't have any problem with them. They were friendly. And it was a good relationship with the population over there. We know everybody. Everybody knew us. And when we was married, everybody from the town was there. We didn't send out the-- the invitations. But everybody knows they going to marry this day-- this day and this. It was on a Tuesday. And everybody was over there. It wasn't separate tables. It wasn't a name tags, who sit here and here. Everybody came in and helped themself, food, then sit down. And there was a-- a band, a guy and a gypsy orchestra. And they were playing. Young people were dancing. We were dancing. And it was a nice, nice wedding. It was after midnight.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you experience anti-semitism after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. When we were in business, yeah. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not-- not really, not really. When was a day in 1956, when was the revolution in Hungary. So a friend of ours came over, a lady, and said, [INAUDIBLE] he has a list. When the revolution over, he will kill all the Jews, some Jews. And you are, and your family, on this list. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we decide-- decided that we leave the country. First of all, I was afraid of death, because it happened. Things like this happened in Hungary. And the second was, I didn't want my children grow up in a Communist country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then we decided that we going to leave Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had children already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Henry was eight, seven-- seven, eight years old. And Marta was six and a half. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before your left, did you have freedom of religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Did we have what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have freedom of religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, it wasn't free [INAUDIBLE] religious in-- in Hungary, by the Communists. But if you was practice religious-- Jewish, or Catholic, or any kind of religious-- you might lose your job. So it wasn't [? wholly ?] religious. But I didn't care. I went to the temple every Shabbos. And [INAUDIBLE] when-- when I could go to the temple. And I had a-- first couple of years, I had my own business. Then they come, and they took my business. And I got a government-- a job at a government store. And I did-- didn't care. I just was practicing for my-- my religion, how much I could. But it wasn't free-- free religion, by the Communists. People-- I'm not talking about Jewish people-- people who want to go to a church some day, they went on different [INAUDIBLE], and they went over there to-- to-- to the church. Because if they were watching people-- and when-- when they saw you go to the church Sunday, you might-- might lose your job.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you decide to leave Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah, what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: But could you describe the revolution?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- we had a radio. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the radio-- we heard in the radio, is a revolution broke out in Budapest. The people occupied the-- the-- the radio station, and so-and-so. And next day on the paper, we saw that. And next day, the Russian came in, or a couple days after-- two days after. Russian came in, and they were marching through the town with big tanks. And they were occupied larger, bigger towns. And that was the revolution. And then we heard this-- this friend of ours told us-- so before, a friend of mine, who had a position not far from the-- from the Austrian border, he came home to see his mother. And he told his [INAUDIBLE] whenever you want to leave this country, I can help you. So when I decided, I went to Budapest. And I went down to this little town, where my friend-- and I told him, it's the time, here. I want to leave with my family. OK. That was Wednesday. Friday-- Monday, yeah. He said, Saturday, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] make sure you be here Saturday. I'm going to pick you by the railroad station at that time, that time. So I came home. It was a Thursday evening. And I told Ilona, we are leaving. We leave everything here, and we are leaving tomorrow.

speaker: interviewer
text: So her sister lived next door, and they come over. We go with you, too. So we make little-- but we couldn't carry much. And in the evening, Friday evening, we went to another town with a-- with a taxi, to other town. And over there we get the train to Budapest, because we didn't want to take the train there. Because we didn't want people know we are leaving. So we arrived in Budapest, and from Budapest to that little town.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our friend was waiting us, by the railroad station. He took us to some friend of his. And we were there till Sunday morning. Sunday morning, a truck came by and picked us up, and took us to somewhere. Somewhere, nowhere. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah, my friend told us, make sure you have a lot of money. How much money you have, make sure you have some money. Because every [INAUDIBLE], you have to pay, have to give some money. So we get some money for the truck driver.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he stopped over there. He say, you see this little house over there, [INAUDIBLE]? You go up in there, and those people will help you. It was in December. We were walking, maybe, an hour. Our group, they were six, about 10 people. Or 12 people, I can't remember. And we arrived to the house. They were really friendly. A old lady-- it was a farm house-- an old lady and his son. And the son was, he was-- he took-- yeah. He was-- he was the one who took people to-- to the border, to Austria. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Can't remember his name. We didn't know his name. He didn't have a name. We didn't ask his name. And that was-- that was about, I don't know, 6:00, 7 o'clock in the morning. But he said, you have to stay here until midnight. And-- but after midnight, we going to the-- So came out there more people, and more people. And so we were about 30 people, [INAUDIBLE] the house. There was a little kitchen. We were in the kitchen, sitting over there, standing over there, because that was the only warm place in the little farm house. This old lady, she was very friendly. She gave us warm milk, to the children. There were several children over there. And gave us bread to eat. And after midnight this younger man-- young man came back and said, everybody get ready. It's time to leave. He, for the baby he said, we'll-- we'll walk. And be very, very quiet. He gave some sedative for the babies. And let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were walking on the open field, and on ditches. Was a big snow. We see-- from the ditches we saw, far away, the-- the towers of the Russian border-- border patrols. So we were walking in the ditches. Suddenly, he stopped us. You see this bridge, far over here? Yeah. You go over there, and pass the bridge. When we pass the bridge, you are in Austria. Then he collect his money-- oh, he collect his money before, in the house. He didn't say so much, so how much you can give. There were people he didn't-- didn't get-- didn't get any money. Was fine. He was a very fine young man. So he left all of us, stranded. And nowhere we think we couldn't do, what to do. What shall we do? So anyway, he left and we started to walk toward the bridge. And we were lucky that there was nobody by the bridge, nobody watched the bridge. But we saw-- far, far-- we saw the-- the-- the border patrol towers, over there. So we went through, went over the bridge. And over there was some-- some people in uniform-- not in uniform. I mean uniform caps. And they told us, we are Austrian college student. And we are here to help the-- the people, the refugees, from Hungary. We are here. And they lead us-- so we hugged them. Was a lot of hugging over there. And they lead us to a school building. And that town-- that town was [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: They lead us to a-- to a school building. There was many people. And there were tables loaded with food. They invited us, help yourself. And we were there during the night. And they were come-- always coming, more and more people. And in the morning, some autobus came by and took us to a big city, Linz, in Austria.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what day did you cross the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in December, between 12, 15, 12, 13-- something like that. Because we left on 9, 10. Probably went to 12 or 13.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the border. Yeah. So they took us to Linz and took us in-- in some camp. There was many, several hundred people over there. Now, over there we felt this anti-semitism, very much. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When we arrived, we get out on the bus. There were standing many people over there-- oh, Jews, Jews, Jews. Did you bring a lot of dollar, a lot of gold? Did you bring a lot of gold from, with you? And so, very anti-semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think when they said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do-- we didn't-- we didn't know what to do. So anyway, we went into the town. Well, I went to the town with another man, and Henry came with me. And we find the Jewish-- the HIAS-- HIAS the Jewish organization. And we reported what happening over there. And they said, we can't stay. We cannot stay over there. It's dangerous over there. They say, there-- there are so many anti-semite over there. So they said, tomorrow morning we pick you up. Tell all the Jewish people to-- to get ready, and we pick you up, and we take you a nice place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So next morning, there was two or three buses. And all the Jewish people-- they picked up all the Jewish people. And they took us to a [INAUDIBLE]. That was a resort place. Every family had a nice-- a nice-- a nice room, furnished. And we were there about two months. And we had a-- we had a nice time over there. [INAUDIBLE] taking care of us. We had good food, enough food, and it was really-- only Jewish people were there-- maybe 100, 150 Jewish people. Then of course, we applied to come to America. Some people there applied to go to Israel. There was-- there was people came from Israel and was asking people, why don't you come to Israel? And so and so. But Ilona had two sisters here in America, so we want to come to America. So we were sending telegrams to Ilona's sisters. And they sent us affidavit. And-- so anyway, when all the papers were in order, then we-- they took us to Bremerhaven in Germany, where we board the-- the ship, a boat to America. What was the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewee
text: that was the name of the ship. There was about 600 people on the ship-- Jewish people, not Jewish people, all Hungarian refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were your children? How old were your children?

speaker: interviewee
text: How old was what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children. How many years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Henry was about eight year old and Marta was six and a half. And took us nine days. After nine days, we arrived to New York. One morning-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one morning the-- well, [? study. ?] We heard some-- some motion, everybody was running here. So we arrived. And we saw the-- through the-- through the early-morning fog, we saw the Statue of Liberty, in New York. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, and that was really a moving experience. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we arrived in-- to America on--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- what did you think, when you saw the Statue of Liberty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we always-- we always thing about it. Now we are free. We are in this free-- this free country, so what-- what we going to do? How we going to [INAUDIBLE]. We were all [INAUDIBLE]. We didn't understand one word, English. We didn't worry about [INAUDIBLE] were worrying how the children going to-- we have to go to school. They going to go to school. And they don't know, they don't understand one word of English. How they going to-- to-- to do it, and everything. So that's what was a big worry for us. But then everything turned out nicely. And then some cousins, then Ilona's sister came. At that time, one sister lived in New York. She came out with her family to see us. And we were in Camp Kilmer for a couple days. Then from New Jersey, came out some cousins, Ilona [INAUDIBLE]. And we got our papers, and they let-- our cousins took us their home in New Jersey. And next day, a cousin-- a friend of my cousin's took me some place, and-- and I got a job. So [INAUDIBLE] that was on a Thursday. And when Monday, next Monday, I was already working in a place. And-- and Ilona was sewing. But so we were working. And-- and the children we took-- went to the school. So it was everything went nice, and went smooth.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll change tapes.
